name: Great Lakes Rugaru / Michigan Dogman
desc: The Rugaru (Ojibwe/Chippewa) is described as a tall, bipedal, wolf-headed being. The Michigan Dogman first documented in print in 1887 (Traverse City Record-Eagle). Highest density modern encounter area. Indigenous oral tradition predates colonial records.

name: Pacific Northwest Canine Cryptid
period: Pre-colonial oral tradition; modern accounts ongoing
desc: Highest density region for modern dogman-type accounts. Multiple Indigenous traditions including Quileute and Quinault describe wolf-people. Overlaps with Sasquatch territory but described as distinct.
refs: Kryptovox corpus — highest encounter density region

name: American Southwest — Navajo Yee Naaldlooshii
desc: The Yee Naaldlooshii (skin-walker) is the most complete behavioural parallel in any Indigenous tradition: watches-then-leaves, felt-known, disclosure cost, self-illuminated eyes. Operates through deliberate transgression of cultural boundaries.
refs: Franciscan Fathers, Ethnologic Dictionary of the Navaho Language (1910)

name: Appalachian Dogman Corpus
desc: Second highest density region. High proportion of accounts with long disclosure delays. Terrain: dense mixed forest, isolated rural communities. Multiple professional witnesses (law enforcement, military).

name: Louisiana Gulf Coast Rougarou
desc: The Rougarou (from French loup-garou) is embedded in Cajun Catholic folklore: breaking Lenten rules for seven consecutive years triggers transformation. Described as having a human body and a dog or wolf head. Still reported in rural Louisiana.

name: Amarok / Bear-Walker (Ojibwe, Algonquin)
desc: The Amarok (Inuit giant wolf-spirit) and Bear-Walker (Ojibwe skinwalker equivalent) traditions. Documented by colonial ethnographers from the 17th century onward. Bear-Walker accounts persist as living tradition in Great Lakes communities.
refs: Schoolcraft, Information Respecting the History of the Indian Tribes of the United States (1851)
Landes, Ojibwa Religion and the Midewiwin (1968)

name: Loup-Garou / Rugaru (French-Canadian)
desc: Rooted in French colonial Catholic culture: missing Easter communion for seven years triggers transformation. Quebec parish records document priests addressing the belief as a pastoral concern. The Métis Rugaru is a distinct tradition describing a large bipedal creature with a wolf or dog-like head.

name: Waheela / Algonquin Canine Spirit
desc: The Waheela of the Northwest Territories is described as a giant bear-dog hybrid. Algonquin traditions across Canada document bipedal canine-headed spirit beings. Wechuge (Athabascan) — a being capable of possession and shapeshifting, specifically canine in its final form.
refs: Hearne, A Journey to the Northern Ocean (1795)
Jenness, The Ojibwa Indians of Parry Island (1935)

name: Nagual / Xolotl
desc: The Nagual is a shapeshifting sorcerer who takes animal form — canine forms are primary. Xolotl, the dog-headed deity of lightning and death, guides souls to Mictlān. Modern Nagual accounts persist in indigenous communities across rural Mexico with strong behavioural overlaps to dogman corpus.
refs: López Austin, The Human Body and Ideology (1980)

name: Huay Chivo
desc: The Huay Chivo (way chivo) is a sorcerer capable of transforming into a fire-eyed goat-man or dog-man hybrid. Self-illuminated eyes are the defining diagnostic feature. Documented in Maya colonial records and persistent in living tradition in the Yucatán region.
refs: Roys, The Book of Chilam Balam of Chumayel (1933)

name: Loup-Garou (Haitian)
desc: The Haitian loup-garou is distinct from the French tradition: a malevolent shapeshifter that takes animal form to attack children at night. Associated with Vodou spiritual practice. Described in multiple colonial accounts as having a dog or wolf-like head.
refs: Moreau de Saint-Méry, Description de la partie française de Saint-Domingue (1797)

name: Lagahoo / Jumbee
desc: The Lagahoo (from French loup-garou) is a shapeshifter described with an animal head, often canine. The Jumbee is a broader malevolent spirit category, with some regional variants described as dog-headed. Documented in colonial ethnographies and living oral tradition.
refs: Crowley, Folklore of Trinidad and Tobago (1956)

name: Lobizón
desc: The seventh son is cursed to transform into a wolf-man on Friday nights. So embedded in Argentine culture that presidents formally became godparents of seventh sons until 2009 (Decree 1183/2009) to break the curse. Described as bipedal, black fur, glowing eyes, foul smell.

name: Lobishomem
desc: The Brazilian Lobishomem (from Portuguese lobisomem) is the seventh-son curse variant rooted in Portuguese colonial tradition. Described as small, agile, and foul-smelling rather than large. Modern accounts persist in rural communities. Colonial Inquisition records document prosecutions for lobishomem belief.
refs: João de Barros, Décadas da Ásia (1552)

name: Barghest / Black Dog / Werewolf Traditions
desc: The Barghest (Yorkshire) and Black Dog traditions span the British Isles. The Padfoot, Shuck, and Gytrash are regional variants — large, black, dog-headed supernatural beings that appear as omens of death. Medieval werewolf accounts recorded in ecclesiastical records. Modern Kryptovox corpus includes multiple UK accounts.
refs: Henderson, Notes on the Folk-Lore of the Northern Counties (1866)

name: Loup-Garou / Beast of Gévaudan
desc: The Beast of Gévaudan (1764–1767) killed 60–100 people in the Margeride mountains. Contemporary accounts describe a large, wolf-like but bipedal creature. The French loup-garou tradition is extensively documented in ecclesiastical and court records from the 12th century onward.
refs: Pourcher, Histoire de la Bête du Gévaudan (1889)

name: Werewolf Trial Records
desc: Germany produced the most extensively documented werewolf trials in Europe. Peter Stumpp (1589) is the most notorious case: confessed to decades of transformation, documented by court records. Stubbe Peeter pamphlet circulated across Europe. Theological and judicial records establish widespread belief at institutional level.
refs: The Damnable Life and Death of Stubbe Peeter (1590 pamphlet)

name: Werewolf / Táltos Trial Records
desc: Hungarian Inquisition records document Táltos (shamanic practitioners) who were tried for werewolf-like transformations. The Hungarian vámpír and farkasember (wolf-man) traditions intersect. Trial records from the 1720s are the most detailed in Central Europe.
refs: Pócs, Between the Living and the Dead (1999)
Klaniczay, The Uses of Supernatural Power (1990)

name: Werewolf Trial Records (Styria)
desc: Styrian court records document multiple werewolf trials. Austrian werewolf belief intersects with the Werwolf and Nachzehrer traditions. Ecclesiastical records from Vienna document bishops addressing werewolf belief as a theological problem.

name: Ulfhednar — Wolf-Warrior Tradition
desc: The Ulfhednar were Norse wolf-warriors who wore wolf pelts and entered battle in a trance state. Distinct from berserkers (bear-warriors). Described in the Vatnsdæla saga and Haraldskvæði. The Norwegian Varulv tradition (shapeshifting wolf-man) is documented in medieval legal codes.

name: Varulv — Swedish Werewolf Tradition
desc: Swedish Varulv tradition documented in ecclesiastical records and folk collections. The varulv is distinguished from the Danish version by its involuntary nature — a curse rather than sorcery. Olaus Magnus (1555) provides the first systematic account of Scandinavian werewolf belief.
refs: Olaus Magnus, Historia de Gentibus Septentrionalibus (1555)

name: Danish Werewolf Accounts
desc: Danish folk and ecclesiastical records document werewolf belief distinct from Norwegian and Swedish variants. The Danish tradition emphasises voluntary transformation through sorcery. Church records from Jutland document pastoral concern with werewolf belief.

name: Pasjeglavci — Dog-Headed Beings
desc: The Slovenian pasjeglavci (dog-headed ones) are among the most specific documented cynocephali traditions in Europe. The Bolhar (1965) text records the unique detail oči obrnjene v tla — eyes turned toward the ground, anatomically unable to look upward. This Tier 3 isolate has no known parallel in any other tradition.
refs: Valvasor, Die Ehre des Hertzogthums Crain (1689)

name: Psoglav — Dog-Headed Demon
desc: The Psoglav (пасоглав — dog-headed) is a demon with a human body, horse legs, and a dog's head with iron teeth. Documented in medieval Serbian Orthodox iconography and folk tradition. Closely related to Byzantine and Greek cynocephali accounts.
refs: Nodilo, Stara vjera Srba i Hrvata (1885)

name: Cynocephali / Kynokephaloi
desc: Greek accounts of Kynokephaloi (dog-headed peoples) begin with Ktesias (5th century BCE) and continue through Byzantine hagiography. St Christopher is depicted as cynocephalic in Eastern Orthodox tradition. Herodotus and later geographers placed cynocephali at the margins of the known world.

name: Volkolak / Oborot — Russian Werewolf
period: Pre-Christian Slavic oral tradition; documented 17th century onward
desc: The Volkolak (вовколак) and Oborot (оборотень) are the primary Russian shapeshifter traditions. Transformation is induced by sorcery or by stepping over an enchanted knife stuck in the ground. Documented in 17th-century ethnographic records and church prohibitions against werewolf belief.
refs: Ralston, Songs of the Russian People (1872)

name: Vovkulak — Ukrainian Werewolf
desc: The Ukrainian Vovkulak (вовкулак) is distinct from the Russian Volkolak: transformation is typically a punishment inflicted by a witch at a wedding, turning the victim into a wolf for a period of years. Documented in 19th-century ethnographic collections.
refs: Drahomanov, Notes on Slavic Religio-Ethical Legends (1876)

name: Ваўкалак (Vawkalak)
desc: The Belarusian Vawkalak shares the broader Slavic werewolf tradition but with local variants: the vawkalak can pass the curse to others through touch. Documented in 19th-century Belarusian folk collections.

name: Benandanti — Friuli Spirit Warriors
desc: Carlo Ginzburg's discovery: the Benandanti of Friuli were a documented society of spirit warriors who left their bodies at night in animal forms to fight witches and protect harvests. Inquisition trial transcripts (1575–1644) provide extraordinary first-person testimony about animal transformation.
refs: Inquisition transcripts, Friuli (1575, Archivo di Stato, Udine)
Pliny the Elder, Versipellis, Naturalis Historia (77 CE)

name: Hombre Lobo — Spanish Werewolf
desc: The Hombre Lobo tradition is documented in Spanish ecclesiastical records from the 12th century. In Galicia, the Lobisomem (from Portuguese) overlaps with the Spanish tradition. Court records from the 16th century document trials for alleged werewolf transformation.

name: Lobisomem — Portuguese Werewolf
desc: The Portuguese Lobisomem (distinct from the Brazilian variant) is the seventh-son curse tradition. Documented in 18th-century Portuguese folk collections and ecclesiastical records. The Roman Versipellis tradition (skin-turner) documented by Pliny (77 CE) predates all medieval Iberian accounts.
refs: Leite de Vasconcelos, Religiões da Lusitânia (1897)

name: Cynocephali — Classical Indian Accounts
desc: Ktesias (Indika, 5th century BCE) is the earliest Greek source for cynocephali, placing them specifically in India. Megasthenes (Indica, c.300 BCE) adds further detail. Al-Biruni (1030 CE) discusses dog-headed peoples in his survey of India. These accounts established the classical cynocephali tradition that spread throughout the medieval world.

name: Shanhaijing — Classic of Mountains and Seas
desc: The Shanhaijing (山海經) documents dog-headed peoples (Quanrong — dog-people) in multiple passages. The Quanrong are depicted as bipedal, intelligent, and inhabiting the northern and western margins. The text also describes the Tiangou (celestial dog) — a large canine being of supernatural power.
refs: Shanhaijing (Classic of Mountains and Seas), various chapters
Mathieu, Étude sur la mythologie et l'ethnologie de la Chine ancienne (1983)

name: Inugami — Dog God / Dog Spirit
desc: The Inugami (犬神) is a vengeful spirit taking the form of a dog or dog-headed entity capable of possessing humans. Inugami-mochi (dog-god keepers) were hereditary families who could send the spirit against enemies — documented in Edo-period court records as a legal matter. The Tono Monogatari (1910) records contemporary accounts.
refs: Inugami possession records, Edo-period court archives (17th–19th century)

name: Aswang — Weredog Variant
desc: The Aswang encompasses a family of shapeshifting beings. The weredog variant is documented in Visayan and Tagalog traditions: a person who transforms into a large, black, dog-like creature at night. Described as bipedal in some accounts. Colonial Spanish records document ecclesiastical concern with Aswang belief.
refs: Ramos, Creatures of Philippine Lower Mythology (1971)

name: Barong — Sacred Canine Being
desc: The Balinese Barong Ket is a sacred dog-lion headed being — the subject of the Barong dance, which is a living religious ritual. Depicted as upright and bipedal in some forms. The ritual represents the eternal struggle between protective and malevolent forces.

name: Bajang — Wailing Dog Spirit
desc: The Bajang is a shapeshifting Malay spirit that takes the form of a wailing dog. Documented in 19th-century colonial ethnographies and the Malay Annals. Dog-spirit possession accounts are widespread across the Malay Archipelago.

name: Shamanic Were-Animal Traditions
desc: Kazakh and broader Central Asian shamanic traditions document practitioners (baksı) who take animal form during ritual journeys. Canine and wolf forms are documented alongside bear forms. 19th-century Russian ethnographers recorded these traditions as living practice.
refs: Basilov, Shamanstvo u narodov Srednei Azii i Kazakhstana (1992)

name: Mongolian Shamanic Encounter Traditions
desc: Mongolian shamanic traditions document spirit beings with animal heads, including canine-headed entities encountered during shamanic journeys. The wolf is a sacred ancestral animal in Mongolian culture; accounts of wolf-headed spirit encounters are documented in 19th-century ethnographic collections.

name: Ghul / Cynocephali in Islamic Scholarship
desc: The Ghul (غول) is an Arabian shapeshifting desert being that takes animal forms including canine. Documented from pre-Islamic poetry. Al-Idrisi's world map (1154 CE) places cynocephali geographically. Islamic scholars inherited and extended Greek cynocephali accounts.

name: Cynocephali in Abbasid Scholarship
desc: Abbasid scholars in Baghdad synthesised Greek cynocephali accounts with Islamic geography. Ibn Battuta's travels mention dog-headed beings in the Indian Ocean region. Al-Biruni's survey places cynocephali in specific geographic locations.

name: Anubis / Wepwawet — Canine-Headed Deity
desc: Anubis (Inpu) is the jackal-headed god of embalming and the dead, depicted standing upright and bipedal — one of the earliest documented bipedal canine-headed beings. Wepwawet (Opener of Ways) is a related wolf-headed deity. The Cynopolis nome (dog-city) worshipped canine deities as primary civic gods.
refs: Book of the Dead (various papyri, c.1550–50 BCE)
Herodotus on Egyptian animal cults, Histories (440 BCE)

name: Buda — Were-Hyena Tradition
desc: The Buda are an artisan caste in Ethiopia believed to transform into hyenas at night and dig up graves. Documented by Portuguese missionaries in the 16th century and by ethnographers into the 20th century. The were-hyena complex in East Africa is among the most extensively documented therianthropic traditions in the world.
refs: Bruce, Travels to Discover the Source of the Nile (1790)
Reminick, The Evil Eye Belief among the Amhara (1974)

name: Buda — Were-Hyena (Eritrean Variant)
desc: The Buda tradition in Eritrea shares the Ethiopian core — blacksmith and artisan castes believed to transform into hyenas — but with distinct regional variants documented in colonial Italian ethnographic records.
refs: Pollera, I costumi e le tradizioni dell'Eritrea (1898)

name: Bouda — Were-Hyena Tradition
desc: The Bouda (بودة) tradition in Morocco describes blacksmiths who transform into hyenas at night. Documented by Moroccan colonial ethnographers and later anthropologists. The were-hyena occupies a specific niche in North African folklore distinct from the werewolf tradition.
refs: Westermarck, Ritual and Belief in Morocco (1926)

name: Ogbanje / Canine Spirit Traditions
desc: Nigerian Igbo traditions document the Ogbanje (troublesome children who cycle between life and death in animal forms). Yoruba traditions include Oya's dogs and canine-headed messenger spirits. Documented in colonial ethnographies and living oral tradition.
refs: Talbot, The Peoples of Southern Nigeria (1926)

name: Akan Canine Spirit Traditions
desc: Akan traditions document spirit beings with animal heads including canine forms among the suman (personal spirit) entities. Documented by colonial Gold Coast ethnographers.
refs: Field, Religion and Medicine of the Gā People (1937)

name: Therianthropic Spirit Traditions (Kongo)
desc: BaKongo traditions document nkisi (spirit objects) associated with canine forms — the dog is specifically associated with the land of the dead and the ability to see between worlds. Documented by colonial missionaries and 20th-century anthropologists.
refs: MacGaffey, Religion and Society in Central Africa (1986)

name: Aboriginal Canine-Headed Spirit Traditions
desc: The Quinkan figures of Queensland rock art (some of the oldest on Earth, some panels dated 30,000+ years) include tall bipedal figures with animal heads. The Bunyip in some regional traditions is described with a dog-like head. Ngurunderi (Ngarrindjeri tradition) involves ancestral beings with animal transformative abilities.
refs: Flood, Rock Art of the Dreamtime (1997)

name: Moehau / Patupaiarehe
period: Pre-colonial Māori tradition; colonial accounts from 1880s
desc: The Moehau (also Maeroero) is documented in the Coromandel Peninsula: a large, hairy, bipedal creature with animal-like features. In Māori tradition, the Patupaiarehe are pale forest beings, some described as taking animal forms. Treated as undiscovered creature in some accounts — unusual among indigenous traditions worldwide.
refs: Beattie, Traditions and Legends as Told by the Natives of Murihiku (1918)
Cowan, Fairy Folk of the Maori (1925)

name: Melanesian Canine Spirit Traditions
desc: Papua New Guinean Highlands traditions document canine-headed spirit beings associated with death and the forest. Documented by colonial Australian anthropologists. Some accounts describe bipedal dog-men as forest guardians encountered by hunters.
refs: Williams, Natives of the Purari Delta (1924)
Seligman, The Melanesians of British New Guinea (1910)
